I've seen a recent post from you that you use a folded table which I have now found. Curious on cheap element objects.

Thanks

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you simply want symbols of the elements, John Gilbert used to recommend a folding fan, a votive candle in a holder, a small glass of water, and a stone. As for how to make use of it, well, that depends very much on what kind of occult work you have in mind. Some schools use temporary altars that are set up in the middle of the room for ritual work, draped with an altar cloth, and then folded up and put away afterwards. Some use permanent altars that are usually over to one side of the space -- they can be the top of a bookcase or a dresser, or what have you. What do you plan on doing?

replies.title: Re: Informing after potential Orders
replies.content:
> turn the scientific method into a spiritual discipline

I have some opinions about this!

There's a super deep rabbit hole there. You can derive the scientific method as a special-case heuristic from a far, far broader cosmological and decision-theoretic perspective that takes algorithmic probability theory (the "classical" kind, the one that doesn't worry too much about "anthropics", "choice of reference machine", or "hypercomputation") as a good starting reference point. Though, empirically, this rabbit hole seems to be a little crazy-making, roughly as much as Kabbalah and for most of the same reasons, for minds that are vulnerable to being crazy anyway. I might even call it a materialist equivalent of Kabbalah, except that its deeper layers are extremely compatible with a negation of straightforward materialism in a way that is hard to avoid, and some people may not cope well with the surprise.

In the context of this broader theory, the idea of controlled replicable experimental tests of preregistered hypotheses can be recognized as being a specific simpler heuristic that is more limited in applicability and less powerful than full probabilistic inference, but also as more robust or "idiot-proof" against imprecise or poorly-considered assumptions, conscious or unconscious exploitation of qualitative judgment errors, and individual or collective motivated reasoning. (Though, as we are now seeing with the replication crisis, apparently not always robust enough.)

The concept of controlled experiments first(?) appears clearly in Bacon's Novum Organum, which is rhetorically motivated heavily by a metaphorical adaptation of Abrahamic abhorrence of idolatry.

You can also relate the scientific method and some related techniques to Buddhist religious ideas about a certain experiential reality which they figuratively describe as the sword of Manjushri cutting off delusions just where they start, though it's not a perfect match, and the Buddhist concept that is rendered as "delusion" here may have some objectionable features from a more world-positive perspective.

I would probably have more to tell you if I had a proper practice myself, beyond just meditations and studies.

> radionics seems like it would mesh nicely with that

Due to properties of our universe that many people would find unexpected, you may have some trouble finding any phenomena that will meet the criterion of controlled and double-blinded third-party public replicability. And so, I recommend kind of strongly against combining that project with any sacralization of that specific conception of what constitutes the scientific method, as things stand.

And on the topic of radionics... I have this idea, not that I speak from experience, but abstracting from what I've read, that anyone who wants to advance the theory behind radionics will probably benefit from knowing a lot of phenomena observed from the study of second-order partial differential equations, and other phenomena from various scientific fields relating to complex purposive reacting mixtures of relatively small, at-least-moderately interchangeable things in complexly structured spaces—especially aquatic microbial ecology, but also physiology, developmental biology, human geography, spatial ecology, and spatial economics. Or, starting further back, it could be useful to learn about phenomena commonly observed in contexts with simple reacting mixtures in discretely structured spaces of internally uniform compartments (linear systems theory, chemical engineering), or simple reacting mixtures in Euclidean spaces with second-order PDE phenomena (pattern formation and parts of statistical field theory, ball lightning, and in some ways laser system engineering and nonlinear optics), or complex reacting mixtures in well-mixed spaces (autocatalytic networks), or reactions that build up, maintain, or break down their own connection structure (mixed-phase fluids, micelles, foams, stochastic process calculi, and in some ways nonlinear resonance), or complex reacting mixtures in moderately complex spaces (cellular biochemistry).

Good evening and thanks as always for hosting this space and answering our questions.

I'm uncharacteristically short on questions this week, just one!

1) Do you believe that modern storytelling has any role to play in adding to the store of spiritually significant myths? If so, do you think it's something that can be done intentionally, and if yes to that, what distinguishes it from regular fiction-writing besides having spiritually significant themes?

Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nobody invents a myth. A story becomes a myth through being picked up and retold until it becomes one of the core narratives of an entire culture. It's possible that some piece of fiction will eventually become a myth, but it's not the writer who makes that happen!  That being the case, I'd encourage people who like to write fiction to concentrate on telling a good story and, if spiritual themes seem appropriate, by all means put them in.

replies.title: Re: Modern Order of the Essenes
replies.content:
1) No, they're two different orders. The Modern Order was founded in the 1950s by Rev, Matthew Shaw of the Universal Gnostic Church, and it doesn't have the New Thought focus of the Order of Essenes founded by Burks Hamner in 1937. The MOE isn't available yet, and it's not next on the list -- John's Golden Dawn offshoot has that status.

2) It should be broadly compatible with both of those. EOTIW is magic, not mysticism -- Knight is precise about such things, and describes it as Christian Cabalistic magic.

3) You'd have to ask a mystic. I simply repeated the description that John always used.

4) Most people I know do it in the morning because cold water tends to shock you awake! That said, I don't know of any reason why the etheric effects would be different.

Hey JMG,

Pretty embarrassed to be that guy on MM this week with another dime-a-dozen “OMG this crazy stuff happened!!” post, but I had two very peculiar nights in a row this week and I was hoping if you could tell me if they just sound like weird static or if they sound more significant. (Keeping in mind I’ve been on a magical hiatus since roughly mid November).

The first night I woke up around 5am with this very intense large sphere of, say, static energy in my upper chest that was slowly trying to force itself up my midline into my head. Wasn’t thinking totally straight, but I felt like it wasn’t in my best interest to have all that energy to burst up the channel into my skull. I tried to just let it dissipate instead of forcing it down, and opened my eyes suddenly if it got too close.

After three times I knew I wasn’t getting anywhere, and called on a personal deity who thankfully shut it all down.

There’s probably a proper name for this that I don’t know, but the second night I woke up in a ‘astral temple’ of sorts. I was aware that I was sleeping, and knew exactly that I was in a strange temple that others have built in the astral field for rituals etc. Was filled with strange icons and statues that I’ve never seen in my waking life, and had a distinct left hand feel in a Hindu sense. There’s a whole lot of important details here that I’m skipping, but I eventually knew I wasn’t getting anywhere with it and chose to wake up.

I work with a shaman at my day job and ran the first night past her quickly. She suspected someone was doing some sort of a working on me. She always err’s on the side of extremely negative though, and to me none of it felt particularly ‘bad’ or foreign etc. Either way, I’m planning on casting a house chart to ask if it was like that tomorrow. Any thoughts or ideas for plans of action if needed are much appreciated.

Cheers

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Ah, the joys of astral static! The magical hiatus may have something to do with this -- you've sensitized yourself, and now without regular banishing the static level is becoming noticeable. One of the basic rules with astral static, though, is that none of it is ever as important as it seems at first.

comments.title: Monsters
comments.content:
Greetings JMG, I just finished reading Monsters and really enjoyed it! It generated some additional questions:
1. Where would you catagorize incubus/succubus? Is this just a variation of the “Old Hag” or something different?
2. Would you consider the Golem a tulpa or something else?
3. Alex Jones would rant about Silicon Valley elites using psychedelics to communicate with aliens. It sounded crazy. Now, it all looks like an elaborate fay practical joke. What implications would this have for those participating in this communication? What do the fay get out of it?
4. There’s a very entertaining YT channel called Dixie Cryptid. It’s got hundreds of audio-only well-narrated stories of Big Hairy Monsters, with a few chimeras mixed in. It’s interesting for two reasons: first, because there are so many stories from all across the world, especially the SE US; and second, because it’s not uncommon for a good story to get 100,000+ views. I can’t quite make a question of out this, but it’s an interesting phenomenon, and your book helped me put these stories in perspective. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Monsters
replies.content:
1) It's something different. The Old Hag phenomenon doesn't have a sexual dimension, while incubi and succubi are sexually predatory by definition.

2) Golem lore is exceedingly complex and there are many different things wrapped up in that body of legend. Some of them are tulpas, certainly.

3) People who are sufficiently ripped on psychedelics quite often think they're communicating with aliens. They also think they're communicating with cartoon characters, teapots, coatracks, and their own toes. Get stoned enough and you can believe anything, no matter how stupid it is. ;-) I don't think the fays have anything to do with it; it's just a bunch of stoners who like to get pretentious about their trips, as stoners often do. "All is love...all is peace...all is one...except the SPIDERS!!!"

4. The Southeast is crawling with skunk apes and other BHM-type monsters, so this doesn't surprise me at all. I'm glad they got a few chimeras too.

replies.title: Re: Monsters
replies.content:
"All is love...all is peace...all is one...except the SPIDERS!!!"

Not the OP, but having had to take care of my stoner brother when he was totally out of it, this is way too accurate....

replies.title: Re: Monsters

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hello, I happen to like tea a lot, and I hope I can help you out a bit here.

The best tea for you depends on what effect you are hoping to achieve in the evening. Starting with the non-caffeinated teas...

If you want to *relax*, as in have your head hit the pillow within an hour of drinking it, you'll want a chamomile tea.

If you want to be calm but alert - activating your parasympathetic nervous system, I think is the technical effect - you'll want a mint tea.

If you want neither medicinal effect above, but merely a warming sensation and a taste I particularly like, you'll want a rooibos tea. The color of it is particularly beautiful.

If you really really need your teas to taste sweet and fruity, instead of herbal, you can get some fruit infusions - if you go with a really high quality one (i.e., actual fruit included) they can be quite good for your health. I've got an elderberry one, sold by local producers in a tin container, which is amazing! The over-the-counter ones include weird chemicals to mimic fruit taste, however, and I'd strictly avoid them.

If you need your tea to have a STRONG and spicy flavor, but still no caffeine, there's a tea called Bengal Tiger produced by one of the main tea companies (whichever one uses no plastic in their packaging) that will really do the trick. I find it to be too much, but several of my friends swear by it.

Finally, if you're one of those people who finds that caffeine relaxes them instead of wires them, there's the endless varieties of black/green/white tea also available. I will only warn that drinking too much caffeine daily, as I did in my younger years, can dehydrate you and cause some damage to the kidneys (Less so probably than the alcohol, though). I'd stick to no more than a cup a night. The amount of caffeine is lowest in white tea, more in green tea, most in black tea. Black tea generally needs milk in it to cut the acidity--unless you really like bitter!

There are almost endless varieties of tea out there, and you can explore them to your heart's content for years to come, but I hope this provides you a basis to start from. Happy drinking! ;)

replies.content:
Hey friend, I'm not JMG but I'd like to offer some advice as someone who's struggled with this issue. His advice above is great and offers a very helpful perspective on this, which gives me some valuable new insight into my own struggle.

Some stuff that's worked for me:

1. Make a new daily habit and keep it. It can be something small and easy that doesn't directly 'confront' the internet problem, like 'I'm going to do 5 body-squats every day' or 'I'm going to spend 10 minutes walking around outside every day.' It's best if you set a time of the day and stick to it. The idea here is to get past the thing that makes you write: "it seems like all the commitements I make with myself systematically fail in a matter of weeks."

When you've got this, you can no longer say that you're incapable of keeping a commitment. This is basically what JMG advises in his will training series; he advises doing something essentially absurd (touch your nose 10 times), but functionally you do something not because you want or need to, not because it's a Hard Thing You Must Do Every Day To Better Thyself, but simply because you've elected to do it and you will it.

2. Identify the which parts of Internet usage drive your engagement. The Internet is a big place - which neighborhoods are you spending your time in and why? Is it social media? Online gaming? Wikipedia holes? Porn? Why do those get their hooks in you? What sort of needs are they satisfying?

One of the really dangerous things about the Internet is that it can trick your brain into believing you're having a fulfilling life, even though something inside is screaming about it.

Social media satiates your social need, video games (especially online games these days, so intentionally designed to hook into the work/reward cycle) satiate your need for accomplishment, wikipedia holes are (imho) the least dangerous, but they are no fit replacement for real study. And porn... well, I could write a lot about porn. For this it's enough to say that it satiates and alters your human drive for love.

Realizing these things was an important step in my battle against this systematic behavior.

3. Find the things that the Internet is supplanting for you and consider ways of satisfying those needs without the Internet. It will be hard, but you did step 1, right? So you've got the will. Apply it.

Join a meatspace club instead of a new subreddit. Go to a nightclub to see pretty people instead of Instagram. Go to the movies (or perhaps a play?) instead of watching yet another video about Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa on youtube. Write a short story instead of playing video games or do what I did which pretty much instantly killed my online video game addiction - join a good DnD table. Good DnD is everything video games aspire to be, except way more enjoyable and creatively stimulating, and you actually make good friends. And unlike video games, which you can pick up and enjoy any hour of the day for as long as you like, DnD is limited by the schedule you arrange with your table.

4. This is the tricky one, and I could be wrong about this. Don't stop using the Internet. If you're under the age of 35, it's almost certainly an integrated, 'essential' part of your life. JMG is right when he says you've got to accept the situation and work with it, rather than fight it and turn it into an opponent. Instead, use the lessons from points 1-3 to maintain discipline, will and mastery over how you engage with it.

The Internet is literally amazing if you are able to stay proactive with it and direct it, rather than being directed by it. Staying in contact with friends hundreds of miles away? Access to primary-source information? Chatting with strangers from different countries? The ability to learn how to do just about any simple task? Insane! Utilize it while we still can.

Best of luck, friend.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Lots of good advice here so far, but I would add two additional subtle things: 1) turn off as many notifications as you can (especially on your phone). These are literal triggers for variable reward conditioning, which builds addiction-like behavior. Push notifications in particular are designed to grab your attention when someone else wants it, rather than deciding where to put your attention yourself. If you have anything that might actually be time-sensitive (like your boss will yell at you if you don't respond to email within a certain time frame), schedule time to check that time-sensitive thing and then go check it out, rather than going to it every time a notification pops up 2) Social media apps built for the phone have a lot more money and effort put into making them addictive than the desktop versions do. So, if you do have something you feel is a legitimate need on a social media platform, check it out on a computer rather than a phone or tablet.

Lastly, as a resource, the book Digital Minimalism by Cal Newport is all about being conscious and intentional in how to use digital technology in your life, rather than defaulting to whatever the services want to push you toward. The short version is to take a month to do a "detox" where you try not to use the internet for anything that isn't required for work, and then find some other meaningful stuff to do instead (as others have recommended here). At the end of the month, see how you feel about re-introducing stuff that you've done without. (This might not be the best starting place if you've been making some efforts and having trouble, but it might be
helpful down the road).

1. Are there any „western“ occult or magic traditions which work with sexual energy?

2. Is sexual energy actually different from other kinds of „energy“ used during magic/occult work, and how? Or is this just a simple name for „the energy that comes up during sexually charged moments“?

3. Branching out into another topic:

I know a lot of „Eastern“ methods of energy healing for the body (e.g. acupuncture/acupressure, meridian work, certain Qi Gong exercises, Chakra healing, …).

Are there any equivalent „Western“ energy healing traditions or modalities?

4. And finally, you cautioned against using e.g. the Lesser Circulation from Tai Chi/Qi Gong together with „Western“ occult/magic practices.

How about Eastern energy healing systems? Could e.g. acupuncture interfere with occult practices?

And specifically: If an energy body is already „primed“ by the Lesser Circulation, and I‘m hoping for it to reverse to the baseline over time, would it then be wise to avoid things like acupuncture for the time being, because they might re-enforce the energy patterns already established by Tai Chi?

Thanks,

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Sexual energy and healing
replies.content:
1) Roughly half of all Western magical traditions do something with sexual energy. The spectrum extends from those that advise celibacy and redirect the sexual currents to other uses, all the way to those that practice ritual sex.

2) My understanding is that it's no different -- it's just that sexual arousal brings up a lot of it in a hurry.

3) Very little of that has survived the persecution of alternative health care by the medical industry, but yes, there's still some of it around.

4) No, acupuncture works with the peripheral meridians to balance the normal flow of qi through the body -- it doesn't redefine the energy flows in a specific way, as the Lesser Circulation does. I've used acupressure (specifically the Japanese form called Do-In) extensively over the years and never found any conflict between that and my magical work.

replies.title: Re: Sexual energy and healing
replies.content:
Thanks.

3. Would you care to give me some pointers on what to search for? Not as medical advice, but I‘m very interested in such things from an academic perspective.

replies.title: Re: Sexual energy and healing
replies.content: Sure. Look into the works of Paracelsus, which have a great deal to do with internal alchemy; the German and central European Rosicrucians; J.-B. Kerning; Franz Anton Mesmer and the whole world of "magnetic healing" that emerged around him before it got gutted and dumbed down into hypnotism; Carl von Reichenbach; and Wilhelm Reich. (No, I'm not choosing German sources because you're German; the German-speaking countries of central Europe preserved this stuff when it was erased elsewhere.)

replies.title: Re: Sexual energy and healing

More generally, there's an astounding amount of first-rate occult literature in German that has never been translated into English, and anyone fluent in both German and English could generate a nice second income by doing translations and publishing them with English or American publishers.

comments.title: Memory Palace
comments.content:
Good morning. I’m currently constructing a memory palace based on the wheel of the zodiac. It’s a great circular room divided into 12 large arches (the signs) with each arch holding 3 smaller arches (the decans). Planetary rulerships, detriments and exaltations and minor dignities are shown by the relevant god by position in arch and their demeanour.
1. I’m using this in the first instance to memorise the zodiac and the various planetary dignities. However I assume once it is committed to memory I can use it as a standard memory palace too. For instance putting the items on a shopping list on each decan image?

3. One of the uses I want to make of it is to memorise herbs for astrological herbal medicine. I can start this easily by hanging herbs (maybe with mnemonic symbol too) on the relevant planet for that herb, so if herbs of mars are indicated I can go straight to the Aries or Scorpio arches to see which might be suitable. However I was wondering if I want to ‘drill down’ into a herbs properties is there a way of opening a ‘sub’ memory palace from that herb image? For instance if I think hawthorn might be a suitable herb I can open another memory palace showing the various properties of hawthorn?
Any advice very much appreciated
Olly

replies.title: Re: Memory Palace
replies.content:
1) Indeed you can.

2) If the things you've memorized are temporary, just clear them away when you're done with them -- for example, if you're shopping, when you put something into your shopping cart, imagine yourself removing it from its place in the memory palace. Then you can restock it later. If the things you've memorized are meant to be permanent, don't copy the palace if you need to memorize something else -- you'll get confused between the two copies. Build another memory palace on a different theme.

3) Sure. In each arch, in the back, have a door that leads into another room. Each room has doors around it marked with something to remind you of the plant, and in each room, you have memory images to remind you of the properties of the plant. You can use non-Euclidean geometry in memory palaces, so the rooms don't have to fit according to any merely three-dimensional plan. ;-)

comments.title: Sage and Smudging

replies.title: Re: Sage and Smudging
replies.content: Okay, let's start with some basics. Magical traditions are not identical around the world and across time; they're culturally specific. Smudging with sage is a tradition used by Native American cultures, and borrowed by some other people from Native American sources. The stuff I've studied has its roots in European cultures -- well, other than hoodoo, which has African roots but has been ebulliently multiracial since about a week after it arrived on this continent. (Hoodoo is to magic, that is, what jazz is to music.) So I'm not the person to ask about what smudging with sage does -- it's not something I do, and the traditions behind it aren't traditions I've studied. You should find a Native American medicine person or someone who's studied with one, and ask them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. The fundamental rule is simple:

DON'T WHINE.

I mean that quite seriously. You maximize the beneficial effect of wretched experiences, in terms of burning off negative karma, by sucking it up and accepting the situation. Fight against it as much as you choose, sure -- as Krishna says to Arjuna in the Bhagavad-Gita, you can work off karma just as effectively by active struggle as you can by passive submission to fate -- but don't throw a pity party for yourself. Whatever situation you're in is the mathematically precise result of your own actions in this and previous lives; you got here under your own power, whether or not that's clear to you at the moment. Accept the situation, respond to it in a willed and ethical way, and if you end up dying horribly, focus your last thoughts on the deity you worship, not on how much it hurts. Your next life will be much better for it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Two observations:

1) This is related to the OSA course. I had an appointment with a therapist today, and we discussed dealing with anger. I used the example of a minor dispute with my partner, and in walking through the event, my therapist helped me understand that the trick with anger is to catch it before it becomes anger, which is much harder than it sounds. I remember noting down to ask the question "WHY am I getting angry right now?" when I get angry in order to figure out whether that anger was actually hurt or disappointment or something else, and why I was feeling it.

And in the case of the dispute with my partner, to also be able to do the same thing but by seeing the situation as much as possible from her point of view. If she is reacting angrily, why might that be? I thought I would share this technique, even if it seems obvious to some.

It strikes me suddenly that this is a practical application of resolving a binary. If I am able to understand my "story" of what is happening during an argument we are having, as well as my partner's, then I have perhaps found the common link necessary to free ourselves from being locked into a binary. In a way, the clarity of understanding is what allows one to lift over or duck under the opposition of a binary. The trick seems to be how to avoid getting caught up in anger, because then it's too late.

I need to test this out on minor situations. After writing this, I think I will go start another argument with my partner... (joking!)

2) I recently finished doing a round of meditation, scrying, and pathworking of the Ogham fews over the last year or so. I am currently spending more meditation time going over the notes I took in my journal, and when I look over them with a "macro eye", I've started to notice a common set of symbols that appear often through many of the scrying / pathworkings.

I'm not sure if this means I haven't fully got in touch with each few, I would have expected each few to have its own unique set of symbols (which is partly the case). But seeing the common pool of symbols was quite a surprise, and is useful to know about.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Excellent. This is what the Octagon Society papers mean by saying that anger is a complex emotion, and usually appears in response to some other emotion, such as hurt or disappointment. Approaching it as a matter of two conflicting narratives, which form a binary that can be resolved with a third factor, seems very productive to me.

2) Also excellent. The common symbols are especially worth meditation, since those belong to the Ogham as a system, rather than to each individual few.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Excellent! Do yourself a favor and read Mary Shelley's novel Frankenstein -- it's better than the Hollywood bowdlerization, more subtle and more interesting. Did you know, for example, that the story begins with a letter from an arctic explorer to his sister in England? And that much of the action of the book takes place on board a ship trapped in the arctic ice?

As for the inner plane dimension of Frankenstein, I don't see that sort of thing as a message, but rather as a vision. Shelley saw, with perfect clarity, where the rising tide of science and technology were headed; she perceived that in the form of a harrowing vision, in which technological humanity ends up being destroyed by its own misbegotten creations; and she sat down and wrote the first-ever novel of science fiction to express that vision. Three centuries later it rings even more true than it did in her time.

comments.title: mysterious neighbor hijinks

The flickering then stopped, seemingly (though I can't jump to this conclusion) after they'd noticed we were still looking at them. From here I went inside and began to write this.

As far as I could tell there didn't seem to be any pattern to the flickerings, but nonetheless I do feel somewhat dazed after the experience. While I am enjoying it, the book appears to work on a philosophical system that reminds me of Plato's Cave. The allegory of Plato's Cave has always unsettled me -- it seems to imply that there are people who cannot handle/comprehend the truth of reality, to whom knowledge is useless. So I end up mired in the question of whether I'm in one group, or another, and if you can tell from inside yourself.

2) A friend of mine has been praying to Aphrodite for stability in his romantic relationships. As you might expect, this did not help, so I recommended that he give offerings to any of the following goddesses for a solution: Hestia, Brighid, Rhiannon, Isis, and Inanna. Are there any other deities that I could recommend?

3) Does it matter what direction a shrine is set in? Currently, my home shrine is set in the northeast corner of my room, but I am thinking of moving it to the southwest corner instead (those are the two available corners, due to some dubious planning choices).

replies.title: Re: Questions from the Cave
replies.content:
3. I have no idea of the universality of this, but in Japan the northeast is in some traditions called "kimon", the demon's gate. Very often in house foundations, "go en" coins (literally "5 yen" but also homophonically "good luck") can be scattered in the dirt under the northeast corner as wards against this bad luck.

Is this superstition? I can't say. You might try divination. Barring other information I'd say go with the southwest, personally.

comments.title: Accidental Cursing?
comments.content:
I think there was a question about someone doing an accidental curse a few weeks ago, though I can't remember the specifics, and it made me think of an incident where I myself might have done something similar.

Some years ago I worked for a computer repair shop owner who was, well, a bit of a jerk (to the point of making demeaning jokes about me in the presence of clients) and also expected me to be able to do things like replace tablet screens which I had neither the training nor talent for (I have Aspergers, and you CANNOT work inside tablets or cell phones with any sort of clumsiness). This man eventually fired me, and it would be three years before I got a job working with computers again-three years in which I basically held this man responsible for destroying my hoped-for IT career. Every time I would ride by his shop (which was fairly often-it was on a main road in the city I lived in at the time), I'd flip it off and scream "Curse you!". Sometimes I'd get a little more creative-"Curse all your computers with the worst Russian cryptovirus ever!" and the like. I eventually moved out of that city and haven't ridden by that business in years, so I'm more able to put it in the past now, but did what I was doing have any magical or karmic effect? And is there anything I might need to do now to clear any karma I may have inadvertently brought on myself?

comments.title: Unusual intuitive experiences and clearing the third eye
comments.content:
Hi JMG!

Recently I've had a development where I've started "seeing meanings" in things. For example, hearing the double meaning of phrases jump out, noticing colors and synchronicities, and also noticing how my choices manifest in the external world. Humorous examples would be deciding to go to a tanning salon and accidentally choosing one with a picture of Saturn on the billboard and colors of Babalon in the booth, or being given an Oatmeal Porter at the bar and ending up in an uncomfortable flirtation with someone a mix of Dirk Bogarde and Mr Rogers.

There are a few things on my mind I'm curious what you'd think:

* General good ways to develop and control it? Make it better but also maybe I should sometimes turn it off because I'm super ungrounded, really these days all I want to do is wander around seeing how the universe responds to my changing eye shadow color, being erratic and "crazy".

* I'm having trouble distinguishing third eye information from projecting my own dark side. A lot of the double entendres that come up seem to obviously relate to my "shadow", taboo things I'm uncomfortable with relating to racial issues, sexual fetishes, violence, misogyny, child abuse, etc. I'm struggling to understand what to do with this shadow information or how to tell when it's real vs projected.

* It seems like what turned this power on was a traumatic work drama that might have been catharsis for a forgotten episode of childhood abuse - or possibly an ancestral mass trauma which in a very light weight way it metaphorically reenacted. Someone told me that that sort of "whole body" experience of doing magic was a form of tantra? It was a fascinating episode and I've never felt more alive but also - hazardous and unhealthy.

Thank you for doing this and I'm so curious what you will think!

comments.title: Being firm on schedule
comments.content:
I’m preparing, or trying to prepare, a business website launch. Members of my family living in the same household are accustomed to having constant access to me, and frequently interrupt me at my work. It’s clear I’m going to have to set a working schedule during which I cannot be disturbed, and be firm about it, even to the extent of locking my studio/office door during working hours. This means a radical change in the long-standing household ecology and family will NOT like it, will most likely deeply resent it and, I am sure, will complain bitterly about it. But if I don’t hold firm my project is apt to fail.

The ideal solution would be for me to have a workspace in a separate building, a shed or garage out back; the inconvenience of going out there alone would be enough to deter most interruptions. But that option is not available.

What practice or policy can I set in place that will protect my working schedule, and have it respected, without screaming or becoming abusive? Perhaps this isn’t a magical question strictly speaking, more of a practical psychological one, but I don’t know where else I could ask.
